core_identity.title: Mean, Median and Mode: Which Average Tells the Fairest Story?
core_identity.author: Gerry Docherty

curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: mean, median and mode (measures of central tendency; outliers and fairness)
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Use and interpret measures of central tendency (mean, median, mode) to describe and compare data sets.

knowing_world.focus: Use a data lab to calculate mean, median and mode, then test how each measure responds when you change values in the data set.
key_concepts: Mean: add all values and divide by how many there are
Median: middle value when ordered (or mean of the two middle values)
Mode: most frequent value (can be more than one or none)
tools: Interactive: Mean, Median and Mode Data Lab (canva_embed): https://www.canva.com/design/DAG7wzY-DXk/vSdWZeBYAHO6MTu1NfUnCQ/view?embed

human_story.focus: Explore how statistics are used to tell stories about people (scores, income, waiting times, performance) and how the choice of 'average' can be fair or misleading.
justice_prompts: Who benefits if we choose the mean instead of the median? Who might be hidden or misrepresented?

expressive_self.focus: Express what you notice about different averages using characters, poetry, or number-line diagrams, and practise explaining the differences clearly to someone else.
activities: Draw three characters called Mean, Median and Mode. Give them personalities (who is most sensitive to outliers?).
Write a short paragraph or poem starting: “When the outlier walks in, the mean…”
Create a sketch showing where mean, median and mode sit on a number line for one of your data sets.
In your own words, explain the difference between mean, median and mode to a friend who hates maths.

designed_world.focus: Use interactive tools to explore outliers (mean vs median) and choose the most honest measure of average for different scenarios, thinking like a data designer.
design_challenges: Activity 2: Explore outliers by adding/removing extreme values and observe how mean and median respond (canva_embed): https://www.canva.com/design/DAG7xDCD9ho/FLIty1u6rzxGS2fvIExrqA/view?embed
Which measure seems more stable when extreme values appear?
Activity 3: Work through scenarios and choose mean, median or mode as the most honest summary (canva_embed): https://www.canva.com/design/DAG7xGbQS9c/3G9FqIcZAhVCmlq_etpiag/view?embed
Buddy challenge: invent a new scenario (steps, game scores, notifications) and justify the best average.
Ethical design: If you were designing a dashboard to show 'average wellbeing' in a school, how would you avoid hiding anyone’s experience?

regulation_supports: Breathe in for 4 counts while silently saying “collect”. Breathe out for 6 counts while silently saying “understand”. Repeat before returning to the tasks.
wellbeing_prompts: What helps your mind-body stay curious rather than stressed when you see averages and comparisons?
How does it feel in your body when numbers don’t tell the full story?

pathways.name: Option A – Class or Fictional Data Set
pathways.focus: Create a short data story from a dataset of at least 10 values, calculate mean, median and mode, and argue which average tells the fairest story.
products: A dataset (at least 10 values), real but anonymised or invented
1–2 paragraphs explaining which average is fairest and why (including any outliers)

pathways.name: Option B – Scenario Critic
pathways.focus: Choose a scenario (from the 'Which Average?' activity or your own) and explain which average is most honest, which could mislead, and how to present the data fairly.
products: Chosen scenario and a clear decision: mean, median, or mode
Explanation of which average is most honest and which could be misleading
Suggestion for how to present the data to keep it fair (e.g., show median + range, mention outliers)

required: Add a list of numbers and divide by a count (mean)
Order data and identify the middle value(s) (median)
Basic understanding of what an outlier is

builds_toward: Outliers and their impact on summaries
Comparing distributions (including box plots as extension)
Using averages responsibly in real-world reports
